title: Trauma Team Activation Resource Locking
description: An acute-care operations workflow that coordinates urgent intake, prioritization, and constrained resource assignment under clinical safety rules. Focus case: trauma team activation resource locking.
assumptions: Each T1 activation_instance is unique and represents one patient/event; predicates on T1 refer only to that activation's lifecycle.
Resources typed as T2,T4,T5,T6,T8,T10,T11,T12,T13 are constrained and must be exclusively reserved (locked) to one activation at a time when assigned.
Availability predicates (e.g., initial R20,R21,R22...) indicate a resource is free; reservation actions move availability from free -> reserved and vice versa on release.
Cross-activation coordination (T17/T18) is used only for service handoffs (e.g., imaging) and not for general resource identity to avoid conflation of activations and resources.
The model treats readiness (prepared and confirmed) as a separate step from reservation — resources may be reserved but not yet ready.
risks: Naming ambiguity risk: if activation-instance semantics are reused as resource identifiers in implementation, automated renaming or mapping tools may conflate resources and activations, producing incorrect assignments.
Deadlock risk: multiple activations reserving overlapping sets of constrained resources could create circular waits if higher-level coordination is not enforced outside these primitives.
Stale reservation risk: reserved resources not properly released (e.g., due to failure to execute release actions) can reduce capacity and prevent real activations from being serviced.
External dependency risk: imaging_resource coordination requires timely responses from external teams; delays or failures in those systems can block activation progression.

Role-resolution risk: coarse staff_role_token assignment (T7 family) may hide distinctions between credentialed provider types; implementations must preserve subtype distinctions (T15,T16) to ensure clinical-scope matches.
